title: The Sixteenth Sunday after Pentecost
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
## Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
## September 9, 2018 | Preacher: Richard Lawson

---

LITURGICAL CONTEXT

September 9, 2018 was the Sixteenth Sunday after Pentecost, placing the congregation in Ordinary Time following the long green season of the church year. Under the Revised Common Lectionary, the readings would have included Proverbs 22:1-2, 8-9, 16-17a, Psalm 125, James 2:1-10 (11-13) 14-17, and Mark 7:24-37, with themes centering on faith, works, and Christ's healing power across boundaries. The Episcopal Church follows the same lectionary cycle, making this a standard Sunday in the post-Pentecost season with no special feast days or commemorations of note.

EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The summer of 2018 had been relatively quiet denominationally, with the previous General Convention having concluded in July 2018 in Austin, Texas, where significant votes on marriage and sexuality issues had just recently been finalized. In the Diocese of Colorado, Bishop Robert O'Neill was in his seventh year as Ordinary, having taken the position in 2011; the diocese was navigating post-Convention implementation of new canonical changes. Saint John's Cathedral itself, located in downtown Denver on Logan Street, was preparing for its bicentennial observances, as the parish would reach 200 years in 2020.

DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver was in the heart of late summer with typical September weather patterns; the week prior had seen regional focus on Labor Day weekend and the approach of fall activities. Colorado schools had just begun their 2018-2019 academic year in early September, with local discussions centered on education funding following the 2018 primary elections held on June 26. The state was heading toward the November 2018 midterm elections with significant competitive races, including the gubernatorial race between Democrat Jared Polis and Republican Walker Stapleton, which was dominating local political coverage.

NATIONAL NEWS (US)

President Trump was continuing implementation of his trade war policies, with tariff announcements and retaliatory measures from China dominating headlines in early September 2018. The confirmation hearings for Supreme Court nominee Brett Kavanaugh had begun on September 3-4, with significant controversy and protests surrounding his nomination following allegations that would emerge later that week. Hurricane Florence was developing in the Atlantic and would make landfall in the Carolinas later that week (September 12-14), becoming a major national news story by week's end. The 2018 midterm elections were less than two months away, with the political environment highly polarized and media focus intense on control of Congress.

INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The Rohingya refugee crisis in Myanmar continued to dominate international humanitarian news, with the anniversary of the 2017 crisis approaching and ongoing displacement of over 700,000 people in camps in Bangladesh. Italy's new populist government under Prime Minister Giuseppe Conte was in its first weeks of governance and already clashing with the European Union over migration and budget issues. The ongoing Syrian civil war remained in its seventh year with Russian and Iranian involvement, though international attention was lower than in previous years as the conflict had become semi-normalized in news cycles.

CULTURAL MOMENT

American culture in early September 2018 was characterized by deep partisan polarization, with the midterm elections creating intense political anxiety and engagement across the country. The NFL season was beginning, with the Denver Broncos preparing for their 2018 campaign amid questions about quarterback Chris Keenum's performance and the team's direction following disappointing 2017 seasons. The broader cultural moment reflected anxiety about democratic institutions, gender and power dynamics (amplified by the emerging Kavanaugh allegations), and economic uncertainty despite nominal strong economic indicators, creating a complex and somewhat fractured national mood.
